title: Anger at Cancel Culture's Misuses
dek: *The machinery of cancel culture runs on a particular flavor of anger - righteous, viral, and strangely unsatisfying. When collective fury replaces nuanced accountability, we lose the very transformation we claim to seek. Here's what happens when anger stops serving justice and starts serving performance, and how to reclaim the fierce clarity that actually creates change in ourselves and our communities.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Cancel culture often weaponizes collective anger without offering repair or transformation, leaving both targets and participants emotionally exhausted. When we mistake public shaming for accountability, we bypass the deeper work of understanding what actually changes behavior. Learning to channel anger toward restoration rather than erasure creates space for genuine growth and healing.

Have you noticed how hollow you feel after watching another public takedown unfold online, even when the person "deserved it"? That emptiness isn't accidental. It's your body registering the difference between anger that transforms and anger that simply destroys. In my own practice, I've watched clients grapple with this distinction - the way participating in collective outrage can feel both strengthening and secretly shameful, necessary and somehow wrong at the same time. The confusion makes sense. We've been handed a blunt instrument and told it's precision surgery. ## When Righteous Anger Loses Its Way Anger exists to protect boundaries and signal injustice. It's the emotional equivalent of your hand jerking back from a hot stove - fast, decisive, protective. Robert Augustus Masters writes extensively about the difference between what he calls "clean anger" and its contaminated cousins. Clean anger, in his framework, knows exactly what it's protecting and stops when the boundary is secure. It doesn't need an audience. Cancel culture's anger rarely stays clean for long. It starts with a legitimate violation - harassment, abuse, harm that genuinely occurred. But then something shifts. The anger multiplies through sharing, quote-tweeting, screenshot threads. Each retelling adds emotional charge without adding clarity. What began as "this person hurt someone" becomes "this person is irredeemable" becomes "anyone who doesn't publicly condemn this person is complicit." Your nervous system knows the difference, even when your ideology doesn't. Clean anger resolves when the threat is addressed. Performative anger feeds on itself, leaving you wired and exhausted simultaneously. You might notice tension in your jaw, a specific heat behind your sternum, the way your shoulders creep toward your ears as you scroll. These aren't signs of moral clarity. They're signs of dysregulation masquerading as activism. The distinction matters because anger that can't complete its protective cycle becomes toxic - to you first, then to everyone around you. When we mistake the performance of outrage for the work of accountability, we end up with communities that feel dangerous to everyone, including the people they claim to protect. ## The Addiction to Collective Fury There's a dopamine hit in group anger that individual anger never provides. When you post something calling out injustice and watch the likes accumulate, your brain registers social bonding, tribal belonging, status elevation. You're on the right side. You're part of the solution. You matter. Beverly Engel's research on shame and anger reveals how often our public rage masks private humiliation. When we feel powerless in our own lives - overlooked at work, dismissed in relationships, struggling with problems we can't solve - participating in collective takedowns offers a temporary restoration of agency. Finally, someone is listening. Finally, there are consequences. Finally, power flows in a direction that feels just. Except it doesn't actually restore your power. It borrows the illusion of power from the crowd. The moment the pile-on ends, you're back in your original helplessness, now with the added weight of having participated in something that didn't actually change the conditions that made you angry in the first place. Over the years I've seen this pattern repeat: someone discovers cancel culture as a outlet for legitimate grievances, finds temporary relief in collective action, then slowly realizes the relief is getting shorter and the anger is getting louder. Like any addiction, you need bigger doses for smaller effects. More extreme positions. Faster judgments. Wider circles of complicity. Your body keeps the score. You might notice you're sleeping worse, startling more easily, feeling a low-grade irritability that colors everything. These aren't signs you're "too sensitive" to handle the truth. They're signs you're marinating in unmetabolized anger that has nowhere to go except round and round in your system. Some people find gentle support in [Bach Original Flower Remedies, Oak - Restore Endurance - Natural Homeopathic Flo](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005P0UPGY?tag=spankyspinola-20) when they're working to restore resilience after prolonged stress, though the real work happens in how you relate to the anger itself. ## What Gets Lost in the Performance Accountability requires relationship. You can't hold someone accountable from a distance, through a screen, with no intention of ever speaking to them again. That's not accountability. That's punishment, exile, erasure. Real accountability looks like this: "You harmed me. Here's how. Here's what repair might look like. Here's what needs to change for trust to be possible again." It requires the harmed person to stay present enough to articulate impact. It requires the person who caused harm to stay present enough to truly hear it. It requires both people to believe change is possible. Cancel culture skips all of that. It goes straight to: "You're out. You're over. You don't exist to us anymore." There's no pathway back because there was never any relationship to begin with - just an audience and a performance. Harriet Lerner, in her work on apology and forgiveness, emphasizes that genuine behavior change requires understanding the specific harm caused, not just the general category of wrongness. When someone is canceled, they learn they're bad, they're toxic, they're irredeemable. They don't learn what specifically hurt someone or how to do differently next time. The public shaming teaches them to hide better, perform contrition better, or double down on defiance. It rarely teaches actual transformation. Meanwhile, the person who was harmed often gets lost entirely. Their specific injury becomes public property, their story flattened into a hashtag. The crowd claims to speak for them, but rarely asks what they actually need. Sometimes what survivors need is public acknowledgment. Sometimes they need privacy, space, material support. Sometimes they need the person who harmed them to actually change. Mass outrage provides none of these with any consistency. ## The Physiology of Unresolved Anger Your body doesn't distinguish between anger at a stranger's tweet and anger at a direct threat to your safety. Both trigger the same cascade: cortisol, adrenaline, increased heart rate, diverted blood flow from digestion to muscles. This response is perfect for escaping a predator. It's terrible for sitting at a computer reading thread after thread about someone's moral failings. When anger doesn't complete its cycle - when you can't fight the threat or flee it or negotiate safety - the activation gets stuck. You might notice it as tension that won't release, a jaw that aches from clenching, a startle response that feels hair-trigger. Some people describe it as "buzzing" or "crackling" just under the skin, a restlessness that makes it hard to settle into anything calm or soft. The NIMH page on emotional regulation outlines how chronic activation of the stress response affects everything from immune function to decision-making capacity. You literally can't think as clearly when you're running on anger fumes. The prefrontal cortex - the part that handles nuance, context, long-term consequences - goes offline in favor of the amygdala's faster, cruder threat assessment. This is why cancel culture conversations feel so binary, so absolute, so devoid of complexity. You're not having those conversations from your whole brain. You're having them from the part that evolved to answer one question: threat or not threat? Across the articles we've published on this site, we keep coming back to this: your body needs to discharge the activation before your mind can process the complexity. That might look like shaking, crying, moving, making sound. It definitely doesn't look like typing more angry tweets or reading more callout posts. Those keep the activation circulating without resolution. ## A Practice for Anger That Has Nowhere to Go When you notice you're caught in the cancel culture spiral - either as participant or observer - try this: Stand up. Shake out your hands vigorously for thirty seconds, like you're trying to fling water off them. Make noise if you can - sighing, growling, whatever wants to move through. Then press your palms flat against a wall and push hard, engaging your arms, shoulders, core. Hold for a ten-count. Release. Notice three things you can see right now, naming them aloud if possible. This isn't about suppressing your anger or pretending the injustice doesn't matter. It's about completing the activation cycle so you can access the parts of yourself that know what actually creates change. Anger that stays in your head and your screen never transforms anything. Anger that moves through your body can clarify what you actually stand for and what actions align with those values. Some people find [Source Naturals Theanine Serene with Holy Basil](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0014H3B3G?tag=spankyspinola-20) helpful for supporting calm focus when they're working to shift from reactive to responsive states, though no supplement replaces the somatic work of actually moving the anger through and out. ## What Accountability Could Look Like Instead Imagine if the energy currently poured into canceling people went toward building structures that actually prevent harm and support repair. Community accountability processes. Restorative justice circles. Mediation. Support for survivors that doesn't require public spectacle. Consequences that connect logically to the harm caused rather than being arbitrarily devastating. These approaches require more work than a callout post. They require staying in relationship with complexity, sitting with discomfort, tolerating ambiguity. They require believing that people can change, which feels dangerous when you've been hurt by people who didn't. But the current system doesn't keep anyone safe either. It just redistributes harm in ways that feel temporarily satisfying to bystanders. The person who caused harm learns nothing except fear. The person harmed becomes a symbol rather than a person. The community fragments into those who condemned loudly enough and those who didn't, with trust eroding in every direction. Real accountability asks: What do you need to feel safe again? What does the person who harmed you need to understand? What conditions would make repair possible? What happens if repair isn't possible - what then? These questions don't have simple answers, which is exactly why the work matters. You can explore more frameworks for this kind of thinking through [our herbal cabinet](/herbs), which includes allies traditionally used for cooling excessive heat and supporting the kind of patience this work requires, or through [more articles in this journal](/articles) that examine anger from different angles. ## When Your Own Anger Needs Tending Maybe you've participated in cancel culture and feel complicated about it now. Maybe you've been on the receiving end. Maybe you're just exhausted from witnessing it, wondering what's real justice and what's performance, what's necessary boundary-setting and what's cruelty with a progressive gloss. Your anger about all of this - about misuses, about manipulations, about the gap between what was promised and what was delivered - that anger is information. It's telling you something about your values, about what you can and can't tolerate, about what you need to be different. The question is what you do with it. You can feed it back into the same cycles that created it, adding your voice to the collective roar. Or you can let it show you what you actually care about enough to build differently. Not just tear down. Build. This might mean stepping back from certain online spaces. It might mean getting more specific about which injustices you have actual capacity to address and which ones you need to let others handle. It might mean having awkward conversations with friends about why you're not participating in the latest pile-on, even though the person probably did do something wrong. It definitely means learning to distinguish between anger that serves life and anger that serves ego. Between anger that protects the vulnerable and anger that performs virtue. Between anger that knows what it wants and anger that just wants to destroy. For nervous system support during this recalibration, some people work with [Gaia Herbs PRO Calm Restore](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0BTMTSSZ6?tag=spankyspinola-20), though the deeper medicine is always in how you relate to your own emotional weather, not in what you take to change it. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### Isn't cancel culture just consequences for bad behavior? Sometimes, yes. Sometimes people face professional or social consequences that directly connect to harm they caused, and that's how accountability works. But cancel culture as a pattern tends toward disproportionate, permanent exile rather than scaled consequences. When someone loses their livelihood over a clumsy tweet from a decade ago, that's not consequences matching harm - that's collective punishment performing as justice. Real consequences are specific to the harm, allow for repair when appropriate, and don't require total annihilation to be effective. ### What if the person who harmed someone refuses to be accountable? This happens often, and it's genuinely difficult. You can't force someone into accountability they don't choose. But you have options beyond either cancel culture or doing nothing: you can warn others in your community about patterns you've witnessed, you can remove your own support or platform, you can build structures that don't rely on that person's participation. Sometimes the most powerful accountability is people quietly choosing not to work with, promote, or enable someone - not because of a viral callout, but because of clear-eyed assessment of who they've shown themselves to be. ### How do I tell the difference between righteous anger and performative anger? Check your body and your intentions. Righteous anger knows what it's protecting and stops when the boundary is secure. It feels clean, directed, purposeful. Performative anger needs an audience, feeds on attention, and leaves you feeling wired rather than resolved. Ask yourself: Am I sharing this because it will materially help the situation, or because I want others to see me as one of the good ones? Both can be true simultaneously, but the honest answer tells you what needs tending. ## The Fierce Clarity That Actually Changes Things There's a kind of anger that burns clean and clear, that sees injustice and doesn't flinch, that protects the vulnerable without becoming cruel. You've probably felt it - that moment when you knew exactly what was wrong and exactly what needed to happen, when your whole body aligned behind a truth that couldn't be compromised. That anger is still available to you. It didn't disappear when cancel culture weaponized its language and mimicked its intensity. It's just harder to hear under all the performance, all the posturing, all the rage that's really fear dressed up as righteousness. The work now is learning to trust your own discernment again. To feel the difference between anger that serves life and anger that serves ego. To know when collective action creates actual change and when it's just theater. To remember that transformation - real, lasting, meaningful transformation - rarely happens through shame, exile, or erasure. It happens in relationship, in complexity, in the hard work of staying present to both harm and humanity. It happens when we stop performing our politics and start living them, when we trade viral callouts for sustained accountability, when we remember that the point was never to destroy people but to build communities where everyone can be safe, seen, and whole. The anger that leads us there won't go viral. It might not even be visible to anyone but you. But it will be real, and it will know exactly where it's going, and it won't stop until it gets there. For more on this kind of work, you can visit [the about page](/about) to understand the perspective we're writing from, or explore the companion practices at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com). ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on anger at cancel culture's misuses, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
